channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: With TPS and Southern Border Decisions, Supreme Court Expands Trump’s Power Over Immigration
item.description: The court handed President Trump victories in his push to rescind deportation protections for hundreds of thousands of people and turn away migrants at the southern border.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s 6-to-3 decision in the two cases divided along ideological lines, with the liberal justices dissenting.

item.title: Supreme Court T.P.S. Ruling on Haiti Plunges Many Migrants Into Limbo
item.description: The decision renders 1.3 million people from more than a dozen countries, many who have lived in the United States for decades, vulnerable to deportation.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan, Andrea Zarate and Jazmine Ulloa
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the National T.P.S. Alliance held a rally in Pasadena, Calif., on Wednesday ahead of the Supreme Court ruling on legal protections under Temporary Protected Status.

item.title: Why the Venezuelan Earthquakes Happened, and What to Expect Next
item.description: Twin earthquakes like those that ripped through the region are unusual but not unheard of. Scientists are already gathering data needed for a more detailed picture.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews
item.media:credit: Leonardo Fernandez Viloria/Reuters
item.media:description: Emergency workers at the site of a collapsed building in Caracas on Thursday.

item.title: Deadly Heat Wave in Europe Continues to Smash Temperature Records
item.description: The continent’s second heat wave since May continued to disrupt daily life for millions. Officials in Spain said it might have contributed to a spike in deaths.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Nazaneen Ghaffar, Aurelien Breeden and Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Oscar Del Pozo/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Construction workers in Madrid this week.

item.title: Latest Victims of France’s Heat Wave: Children Locked in Family Cars
item.description: As temperatures pass record levels in France, the heat wave-related death toll continues to rise.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Joann Pai for The New York Times
item.media:description: Along the seine in Paris on Wednesday.

item.title: See Record-Breaking Extreme Heat Wave Across Europe in Photos
item.description: Temperature records have been broken in several European countries this week.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: A woman cooling herself with a hand-held fan during the heat wave in Paris, on Thursday.

item.title: Inflation Accelerated in May as Iran War Pushed Up Prices
item.description: A closely watched measure of inflation ticked up as the conflict in the Middle East inflated energy prices.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Gasoline prices are up more than 30 percent since the start of the war in Iran.

item.title: U.S. Inflation Problems Are Far From Over
item.description: The Federal Reserve’s new chairman has vowed to deliver price stability, but officials are at odds over whether that will require higher borrowing costs.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Ben Casselman
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the new chairman of the Federal Reserve, has expressed interest in examining how it measures and models price pressures.

item.title: Chinese A.I. Models Gain Ground on Anthropic and OpenAI
item.description: Silicon Valley engineers recently flocked to new technology from a Chinese company, Z.ai, that is almost as good as its American competitors but much cheaper.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz, Karen Weise and Meaghan Tobin

item.title: Big Companies Aim to Ease A.I. Transition for American Workers
item.description: OpenAI, Anthropic, Amazon and Microsoft have signed on to an effort led by Gina Raimondo, a former commerce secretary.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gina Raimondo, a former commerce secretary and Rhode Island governor, is calling for companies and the government to do more to orient American workers in a new A.I. era.

item.title: IBM Unveils New Method to Make Smaller Computer Chip Parts
item.description: Industry leaders had worried that innovations in chip miniaturization were no longer possible.
item.dc:creator: Don Clark
item.media:credit: Bryan Derballa for The New York Times
item.media:description: A silicon wafer full of microchips at the IBM research facility in Albany, N.Y. Industry analysts briefed on an IBM announcement were impressed, while noting that the approach would face competition.

item.title: Apple Raises Prices on Macs and iPads Amid the A.I. Boom
item.description: The tech giant cited the soaring costs of memory and storage chips as it increased prices more than $200 on some devices.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Even with a sprawling supply chain, Apple is not immune to the global shortage of chips.

item.title: Federal Judge Strikes Key Parts of Trump Order Restricting Mail Voting
item.description: The ruling rejected the Trump administration’s attempt to change federal election procedures through an executive order.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti and Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: “The Constitution does not grant the President any specific powers over elections,” a federal judge ruled on Thursday, tossing crucial parts of President Trump’s executive order that sought to restrict mail voting.

item.title: Lesson Learned From Iran War: Reduce Reliance on Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Increased stockpiles, more pipelines and other tactics could loosen the waterway’s stranglehold on the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: A vessel in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman, this week.

item.title: Iraq’s New Leader Faces Resistance Trying to Bring Militias Under State Control
item.description: The Trump administration has demanded that Iraqi leaders distance themselves from Iran and rein in Iran-linked militias that are outside of government control.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim and Falih Hassan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ahmad Al-Rubaye/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Peace Brigades militia during a ceremony in the city of Samarra, Iraq, in June, marking their separation from the Sadrist movement and their integration into the Iraqi security forces.

item.title: This Doctor Can Change the Color of Your Eyes. Should He?
item.description: Francis Ferrari, a French ophthalmologist, invented a controversial procedure that can change eye color. People from around the world are willing to take the risk.
item.dc:creator: Saskia Solomon and Elliott Verdier

item.title: Christopher Nolan on the Anxiety of Filming ‘The Odyssey’ in IMAX
item.description: Filming entirely in IMAX meant new engineering and actor ingenuity. It was only halfway through the six-country shoot that he felt he could pull it off.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Erik Tanner for The New York Times
item.media:description: After reading “more translations than I care to remember,” Christopher Nolan realized “The Odyssey” was his ur-text: “It’s in everything I’ve done before.”

item.title: Why These Affordable Homes Face a 31% Rent Increase
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani has promised relief for rent-stabilized tenants. But thousands of apartments under a separate city program are subject to increases, creating an awkward contrast.
item.dc:creator: Mihir Zaveri and Dana Rubinstein
item.category: Department of Housing Preservation and Development (NYC)
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tracey Towers in the Bronx is part of the Mitchell-Lama program, which created a decades-old bastion of middle-income and working-class housing in New York City.

item.title: Mamdani’s Rent Freeze Could Become Reality Today. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: A freeze for rent-stabilized homes is expected when a city panel votes on Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s plan on Thursday, but the move is likely to face legal challenges.
item.dc:creator: Mihir Zaveri
item.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s pledge to “freeze the rent” for rent-stabilized apartments was a popular rallying cry during his mayoral campaign.

item.title: Mamdani Ends Day Care Contract With Eric Adams’s Former Romantic Partner
item.description: The city had accused Jasmine Ray of fiscal mismanagement. She said that she had been unfairly targeted because of her ties to the former mayor.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Haag
item.category: Mayor's Office of Sports, Wellness and Recreation (NYC)
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Cornerstone Day Care Center, which opened in 1966, is among the oldest day cares in Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn.

item.title: Representative Tom Kean, Missing for Months, Is Back Home in New Jersey
item.description: The congressman, who has been absent from Washington since March, answered the door of his home on Wednesday evening. He was wearing a suit and tie.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr., absent from Congress for nearly four months, was back at his New Jersey home on Wednesday evening.

item.title: Mamdani’s Big Wins May Be a Headache for the Governor Who Has Backed Him
item.description: Gov. Kathy Hochul has worked with Mayor Zohran Mamdani on child care and second-home taxes. But the triumphs of democratic socialists on Primary Day could complicate her vision.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Shannon Stapleton/Reuters
item.media:description: Gov. Kathy Hochul celebrated the victory of Micah Lasher, an establishment candidate in the Democratic primary for the 12th Congressional District.

item.title: Here’s What It Means to Be a Democratic Socialist
item.description: Universal health care, taxing the wealthy and opposition to military aid to Israel are among the movement’s key tenets.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Democratic Socialists of America helped organize a “Tax the Rich” rally in February in Albany, N.Y.

item.title: After Banner Night, D.S.A. Sharpens Its Policy and Political Ambitions
item.description: After democratic socialists captured two House primaries and several state legislative contests in New York, the group’s leaders mulled how to use their expanding influence.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes and Jeffery C. Mays
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
item.media:description: A rally to push the Tax the Rich movement was held in Albany earlier this year, but Mayor Zohran Mamdani did not attend.

item.title: Bible Passages May Soon Be Required Reading in Texas Public Schools
item.description: Texas is set to pass what may be the first state-mandated book list for public school students. It focuses on classic literature and includes Bible excerpts.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Mervosh
item.media:credit: Sandy Huffaker for The New York Times
item.media:description: Most book lists are set by individual teachers or schools.

item.title: How Honda’s Pledge to Go All-Electric Unraveled
item.description: A derailed gamble led Honda to its first-ever annual loss and has intensified scrutiny over the future of its chief executive.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:credit: John Locher/Associated Press
item.media:description: Honda was developing several next-generation electric vehicles that were ultimately scrapped because of declining global E.V. demand.

item.title: One-Party Rule Is Hiding in Plain Sight
item.description: Why does our seemingly competitive political system produce so much stagnation and corruption?
item.dc:creator: David French

item.title: Don the Builder
item.description: We look at Trump’s construction projects in Washington, D.C.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump during a tour of the White House ballroom construction project in May.
